operations.rationale: Replace the reader-visible code-style metric block with prose while preserving the canonical AI/research formulas.
content_lines: Standard autoregressive decoding, raw throughput benchmarks, and isolated serving optimizations do not specify when a faster generation mode is acceptable, how it is verified, or how its failures block promotion.

The failure is conceptual before it is technical. A model can emit many tokens quickly and still produce less useful cognition per second if more tokens are rejected, repaired, rerun, or audited. A serving system can improve aggregate throughput while leaving single-request latency unchanged. A speculative or diffusion system can look fast when proposed tokens are counted, then lose the advantage when accepted tokens, verifier cost, memory pressure, retries, and task success are counted.

The accounting unit has to be stricter than tokens per second. The stack should care about accepted or verified correct tokens per wall-clock time, and about task success per wall-clock time, under a declared task and risk model. Those metrics are still incomplete, but they point the architecture toward useful governed output rather than surface text velocity.

This is why a generation mode cannot be promoted just because it is fast in isolation. It must still say what was proposed, what was accepted, what failed, what was repaired, what fell back to a slower path, and whether the whole governed route delivered correct work sooner.

operations.rationale: Turn the generation-mode taxonomy table into reader prose while preserving the canonical AI/research comparison matrix.
content_lines: The taxonomy is deliberately plural because generation speed is not one problem. The autoregressive baseline remains the fallback and measurement anchor: it is slow, but it gives the stack a known left-to-right route. Multi-token prediction, speculative decoding, multi-head internal drafting, and feature-level or latent drafting all try to propose more future work from a cheaper path, but their proposed output only matters when acceptance, rejection, verifier cost, and fallback are recorded separately.

Lookahead and trie retrieval reuse likely continuations from prior patterns, which can help only when stale procedural-memory shortcuts are controlled. Diffusion language modeling sketches or denoises many positions in parallel, but the stack still has to verify span commitments, uncertainty repair, and final task success. Multi-seed diffusion and hybrid autoregressive, MTP, and diffusion routes remain research directions here until useful solution per second improves after verifier and parallel-compute costs are counted.

Early-exit and self-speculative inference use intermediate layers as cheap drafts and deeper layers as verifiers. State-space or recurrent alternatives attack sequence-processing cost through a different substrate. KV-cache and serving-layer accelerators improve memory layout, batching, and throughput around generation. These methods can compose, but their evidence records cannot collapse into one speed claim.

Generation governance must keep decoding tricks, training objectives, serving memory systems, and sequence-model substrates separate. Each solves a different bottleneck. A faster route becomes useful only when the record shows which bottleneck improved and which quality, memory, verification, or fallback cost moved elsewhere.
